content.text: A chaos experiment that produces findings but no action items is a performance, not a practice. The learning value of chaos engineering is not in the experiment itself but in the systematic conversion of surprising observations into permanent improvements to the system's resilience, the team's runbooks, and the CI test suite. For agentic AI systems, where failure modes include not just infrastructure failures but model behaviour surprises, this conversion process requires a classification framework and tracking discipline that most teams do not have in place.

Chaos experiment learning and action items refers to the structured process by which the observations from a chaos experiment — what happened that was not expected, what did not happen that was expected, what operational surprises emerged — are converted into a classified set of findings, each linked to a prioritised action item with an owner, due date, and success criterion, and eventually into permanent improvements to the system, runbooks, and CI test suite.

The process has five phases: hypothesis definition (what behaviour is expected under the injected failure), experiment execution (inject the failure, observe the system), analysis (compare actual to expected behaviour, classify each discrepancy), action item tracking (create tickets for each finding, assign owners and deadlines), and knowledge base update (publish the experiment report, update runbooks, add CI regression tests).

For agentic AI systems, the finding classification must include AI-specific categories beyond standard infrastructure chaos findings: model fallback behaviour (did the agent correctly fall back to a cached response or simpler model when the primary LLM was unavailable?), guardrail engagement (did safety and constraint guardrails engage correctly under degraded conditions?), agent state recovery (did the agent resume from the correct checkpoint after a simulated crash?), and tool-call error handling (did the agent correctly handle timeout and malformed responses from tool services?).

Most chaos engineering programs produce experiments and reports but fail at the action item conversion step. Findings are documented but not tracked; runbooks are flagged for update but not updated; CI regression tests are discussed but not written. The result is a chaos program that feels productive — experiments run, reports are written — but produces no durable improvement in system resilience.

The failure mode is organisational, not technical: without a structured process for converting chaos findings into tracked action items with SLA accountability, the natural outcome is that findings sit in reports that are read once and forgotten. The team's next chaos experiment rediscovers the same gaps that the previous experiment found but did not fix.

For agentic AI systems, the stakes of this failure are higher than for standard services. A web server that fails to handle a 503 from a database returns an error to the user. An agent that fails to handle a 503 from an LLM provider may hang indefinitely while holding a tool lock, replay tool side effects after crashing and recovering from the wrong checkpoint, or return a partially complete response that leaves the user's workflow in an inconsistent state. These failure modes are subtle, expensive to debug, and preventable with the discipline of acting on chaos findings.
Systematic action item tracking converts one-time experiment findings into permanent code, runbook, and CI improvements.
Every chaos finding that was not caught by CI tests becomes a new CI regression test. The test suite grows with each experiment, ensuring that fixed weaknesses do not regress.
Operational surprises discovered in chaos experiments (alert not firing, runbook step ambiguous) are corrected before the same gap is encountered in a real incident.
Experiment reports and the action item trail constitute an institutional knowledge base of the system's failure modes and how they were addressed.
Priority-based action item SLAs (P0: 1 week, P1: 1 month) create accountability for completing improvements rather than leaving them as backlog items indefinitely.
A chaos program that runs monthly experiments will produce 5-15 action items per experiment. Without discipline in closing items, the backlog grows faster than the team can address it.
Verifying that an action item resolved the finding requires re-running the experiment, which carries the same cost and risk as the original experiment.

Chaos action items compete with feature development for engineering capacity. P2 and P3 items frequently get deprioritised indefinitely unless the chaos program has explicit capacity allocation in sprint planning.
Detailed experiment reports that are not acted on create report fatigue — the team loses confidence that the reports produce value, and participation in the chaos program declines.

Each chaos experiment produces an experiment report containing a structured set of findings. Each finding documents the expected behaviour (from the hypothesis), the actual behaviour observed during the experiment, the root cause of the gap, a classification into one of four categories (resilience gap, recovery failure, operational surprise, or hypothesis confirmed), a priority level (P0-P3), and an action item with owner and due date.

Findings classified as P0 (agent state corruption, safety guardrail failure, data loss scenario) require an action item completed within one week and immediate escalation to engineering leadership. P1 findings (significant resilience gap, SLO breach under the tested failure scenario) require completion within one month. P2 findings (suboptimal behaviour that degrades user experience but does not breach SLO) require completion within one quarter. P3 findings (minor operational friction, nice-to-have runbook improvements) are added to the backlog without a firm deadline.

Action items are tracked in the team's issue tracker with a label that links them to the originating chaos experiment. At each monthly chaos review, open action items are reviewed for SLA compliance. Items past their SLA are escalated to engineering leadership unless they have an approved extension with a new due date. Items that are completed are verified by running a targeted version of the original experiment before being closed.

attrs.alt: Chaos experiment learning lifecycle: 5-phase cycle from hypothesis through knowledge base update, with AI-specific finding classification

content.text: The five-phase lifecycle converts experiment observations into classified findings, tracked action items, and permanent system improvements. The finding classification framework distinguishes between resilience gaps, recovery failures, operational surprises, and confirmed hypotheses. Knowledge base outputs (experiment reports, runbook updates, CI tests) constitute the durable learning record.

The structured learning process is appropriate for any chaos engineering program running in production or production-like environments. In non-production environments (staging, pre-production), a lighter process is appropriate: findings are captured but may not require formal priority classification and SLA tracking.

The process is most valuable when the chaos program is mature enough to have accumulated 5+ experiments. At this point, patterns emerge across experiments — the same category of gap (e.g., LLM provider fallback not working) appears in multiple experiments — and the action item tracking produces insight into which resilience dimensions the team is consistently not addressing.

For new chaos programs (first 1-3 experiments), the process overhead of formal classification and SLA tracking may be premature. Start with a lighter structure: document findings in a shared document, assign one owner per finding, and review at the next sprint planning.

# chaos/experiment_report.py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

FindingCategory = Literal["resilience_gap", "recovery_failure", "operational_surprise", "confirmed"]
Priority = Literal["P0", "P1", "P2", "P3"]

@dataclass
class ChaosFinding:
    id: str
    category: FindingCategory
    priority: Priority
    description: str
    expected_behaviour: str
    actual_behaviour: str
    action_item: str | None
    owner: str | None
    due_date: date | None
    resolution_status: Literal["open", "in_progress", "resolved", "wont_fix"] = "open"
    re_experiment_required: bool = True

@dataclass
class ChaosExperimentReport:
    experiment_id: str
    experiment_name: str
    date: date
    environment: str
    hypothesis: str
    failure_injected: str
    blast_radius: str
    duration_minutes: int
    steady_state_before: dict
    steady_state_after: dict
    findings: list[ChaosFinding] = field(default_factory=list)
    runbook_updates_required: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    ci_tests_to_add: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def open_p0_p1_items(self) -> list[ChaosFinding]:
        return [f for f in self.findings
                if f.priority in ("P0", "P1") and f.resolution_status == "open"]

    def to_markdown(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"# Chaos Experiment Report: {self.experiment_name}",
            f"**Date:** {self.date} | **Environment:** {self.environment}",
            f"**Failure injected:** {self.failure_injected}",
            f"**Blast radius:** {self.blast_radius}",
            "",
            "## Hypothesis",
            self.hypothesis,
            "",
            "## Findings",
        ]
        for f in self.findings:
            lines.append(
                f"### [{f.priority}] {f.id}: {f.description} ({f.category})"
            )
            lines.append(f"- **Expected:** {f.expected_behaviour}")
            lines.append(f"- **Actual:** {f.actual_behaviour}")
            if f.action_item:
                lines.append(f"- **Action:** {f.action_item} ({f.owner}, due {f.due_date})")
            lines.append("")
        return "\n".join(lines)
Track action items and SLA compliance

# chaos/verify_fix.py
"""
Re-runs a specific chaos experiment to verify that an action item resolved the finding.
Links the verification result to the original finding in the report.
"""
import subprocess, json, datetime
from pathlib import Path

def run_chaos_experiment(
    experiment_id: str,
    failure_type: str,
    target_service: str,
    duration_seconds: int = 120,
) -> dict:
    """Run a chaos experiment via Chaos Mesh or Litmus and return the result."""
    # Example: Chaos Mesh network partition
    chaos_manifest = {
        "apiVersion": "chaos-mesh.org/v1alpha1",
        "kind": "NetworkChaos",
        "metadata": {"name": f"verify-{experiment_id}"},
        "spec": {
            "action": "partition",
            "mode": "one",
            "selector": {"labelSelectors": {"app": target_service}},
            "duration": f"{duration_seconds}s",
        }
    }
    # Apply and observe
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "apply", "-f", "-"],
        input=json.dumps(chaos_manifest),
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    # ... observe metrics and return result
    return {
        "experiment_id": experiment_id,
        "failure_type": failure_type,
        "verified_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "status": "passed" if result.returncode == 0 else "failed",
    }

def mark_finding_resolved(reports_dir: str, experiment_id: str, finding_id: str, verification: dict):
    path = Path(reports_dir) / experiment_id / "report.json"
    report = json.loads(path.read_text())
    for finding in report["findings"]:
        if finding["id"] == finding_id:
            finding["resolution_status"] = "resolved" if verification["status"] == "passed" else "open"
            finding["verification"] = verification
    path.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
Convert findings to CI regression tests

# tests/resilience/test_agent_llm_provider_failure.py
"""
Chaos experiment finding converted to a CI regression test.
This test was generated from experiment EXP-2024-007 finding F-003:
'Agent did not fall back when LLM provider returned 503.'
"""
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from agent.core import AgentSession
from agent.exceptions import LLMProviderError

@pytest.mark.resilience
def test_agent_falls_back_on_llm_503():
    """Agent must use cached fallback response when LLM returns 503."""
    mock_llm = MagicMock()
    mock_llm.complete.side_effect = LLMProviderError(503, "Service Unavailable")
    mock_cache = MagicMock()
    mock_cache.get.return_value = {"response": "cached fallback", "cached_at": "2024-01-01"}

    with patch("agent.core.llm_client", mock_llm), \
         patch("agent.core.response_cache", mock_cache):
        session = AgentSession(user_id="test_user")
        result = session.process_turn("What is the status of my order?")

    assert result["source"] == "cache"
    assert result["response"] == "cached fallback"
    mock_cache.get.assert_called_once()

@pytest.mark.resilience
def test_agent_retries_with_backoff_on_429():
    """Agent must retry with exponential backoff on rate limit errors."""
    call_count = 0
    def side_effect(*args, **kwargs):
        nonlocal call_count
        call_count += 1
        if call_count < 3:
            raise LLMProviderError(429, "Rate limit exceeded")
        return {"response": "success", "usage": {"tokens": 100}}

    mock_llm = MagicMock()
    mock_llm.complete.side_effect = side_effect

    with patch("agent.core.llm_client", mock_llm):
        session = AgentSession(user_id="test_user")
        result = session.process_turn("Summarize this document.")

    assert result["response"] == "success"
    assert call_count == 3   # failed twice, succeeded on third

Experiment EXP-2024-007: Injected LLM provider outage (return 503 for 5 minutes). Expected: agent falls back to cached response within 10 seconds. Actual: agent hung for 30 seconds, then threw an unhandled exception that terminated the session without returning a user-facing error. Finding: P1 resilience gap. Action item: implement LLM provider fallback with cached response, add timeout wrapper to all LLM calls. Due: 4 weeks. Verification: re-run experiment after fix; agent must return cached response within 10 seconds. CI regression test: test_agent_falls_back_on_llm_503.

Experiment EXP-2024-011: Injected tool service latency (500ms additional latency on all tool responses for 10 minutes). Expected: agent continues processing within SLO with graceful degradation. Actual: P95 TTFT increased from 1.8 to 6.2 seconds, breaching the 2.0 second SLO. Finding: P1 resilience gap. Root cause: tool calls are sequential, not parallel; cumulative latency of 5 tool calls each with 500ms added = 2.5 seconds additional per agent turn. Action item: parallelize independent tool calls. Due: 6 weeks.

A financial services firm running an AI compliance checking agent established a monthly chaos engineering program with a structured learning process. After 6 months, the action item tracker revealed a pattern: 7 of 18 open action items were in the 'agent state recovery' category — scenarios where the agent resumed from a checkpoint but replayed idempotent tool calls (document tagging, compliance flag setting) because the checkpoint did not record which tool calls had been completed.

The pattern identified a systemic architectural gap: the agent's checkpoint mechanism recorded conversation state but not tool execution state. A single architectural fix — extending the checkpoint record to include completed tool call IDs with their results — closed 7 action items simultaneously. This insight would not have been visible without the action item tracking and category classification that the structured learning process produced.

Before the fix was deployed, the team wrote 7 CI regression tests (one per closed action item) that verified the agent correctly skipped already-completed tool calls when recovering from a checkpoint. The tests were added to the resilience test suite and now run on every PR that touches the checkpoint or tool execution code.
Every finding classified as a resilience gap or recovery failure must have an action item. A finding without an owner and due date will not be addressed.
Action items not in the issue tracker:
Chaos action items documented only in the experiment report (a document) rather than in the issue tracker are invisible to sprint planning and will be deprioritised indefinitely.
No CI test for fixed findings:
Closing an action item without writing a CI regression test means the fix may regress without detection. Every P0 and P1 finding resolution must produce a test.
Re-experiment with the same blast radius as original:
The re-experiment to verify a fix should be scoped to the minimum blast radius needed to verify the specific finding, not a full re-run of the original experiment.
Classification by technical category rather than resilience impact:

Finding classification should reflect resilience impact (can the system tolerate this failure?) rather than technical category (what failed?). A minor infrastructure failure that causes agent state corruption is a P0 finding regardless of its technical category.
Write the experiment report before closing the experiment:
Analysis and classification quality degrades when done days or weeks after the experiment. Document findings while the experiment context is fresh.
Classify findings in the experiment debrief (not async):
Hold a 30-minute debrief within 24 hours of each experiment where the team classifies findings and assigns action items. Async classification produces inconsistent priorities and unassigned items.
Review action item SLA compliance monthly:
Include open chaos action items in the monthly capacity and reliability review. SLA breaches are escalated to engineering leadership.
Use finding category patterns to identify systemic gaps:

Quarterly, review the distribution of finding categories across all experiments. A high proportion in one category (e.g., recovery failures) indicates a systemic resilience gap that requires architectural attention.
Plan experiments 30-60 days in advance and publish the schedule to the team. Unplanned experiments with short notice produce poor hypothesis definition and inadequate monitoring setup.

The chaos learning process does not add latency to any production path — all processing is offline, post-experiment. The action item tracking overhead is proportional to the number of experiments per month multiplied by the average finding count. A program running 2 experiments per month with an average of 8 findings each produces 16 action items per month and requires a weekly 30-minute action item review.

The CI regression test suite grows with each closed action item. After 12 months of a monthly chaos program, the suite may contain 50-100 resilience-specific tests. These tests are slower than unit tests (they simulate failure conditions) and should be segregated into a separate test target that runs on a schedule (nightly or on every PR touching resilience-relevant code) rather than blocking the main CI pipeline.
Kubernetes-native; supports network, pod, memory chaos
Alternative to Chaos Mesh; good observability integration
Verify alerts fire within expected time during experiment

Chaos experiments inject real failures into real systems. The experiment report and action item tracker contain information about the system's vulnerabilities — failure modes, unhandled error paths, misconfigured guardrails. This information must be protected with the same access controls as security vulnerability disclosures.
Restrict experiment reports to the SRE team and engineering leadership. Reports contain vulnerability information that could be useful to an attacker.
P0 and P1 action items that describe unaddressed vulnerabilities should not be visible to all employees through a public issue tracker.
All experiments injecting failures into production must be approved by two senior engineers and the on-call manager before execution. The approval must document the abort criteria.

Implement automated experiment abort: if a defined SLO metric (P95 TTFT, error rate) breaches a threshold during the experiment, the experiment is automatically terminated and the failure injection reversed.

Think of the chaos experiment learning process as the engineering equivalent of a pre-mortem combined with a surgical debrief. Before the experiment, the team performs a pre-mortem: what could go wrong, what do we expect to happen, what is the blast radius if it goes wrong. After the experiment, the team performs a surgical debrief: exactly what happened, was it what was expected, what was surprising, and what must be changed before this failure mode is encountered for real.

The action item is the commitment that comes out of the debrief. Without the commitment — with owner, due date, and success criterion — the debrief is analysis without consequence. The CI regression test is the verification that the commitment was fulfilled. Without the test, the fix may regress silently. The report is the institutional memory that prevents the team from re-discovering the same gaps in future experiments.

Every chaos finding classified as a resilience gap or recovery failure must produce a tracked action item with an owner, due date, and success criterion — findings without action items will not be addressed.

Agentic AI chaos experiments require an AI-specific finding classification that includes model fallback behaviour, guardrail engagement, and agent state recovery — categories that standard infrastructure chaos programs do not cover.
P0 findings (safety guardrail failure, agent state corruption, data loss) require a one-week resolution SLA and immediate escalation to engineering leadership.
Every closed P0 and P1 action item must produce a CI regression test before the finding is marked resolved; without a test, the fix may regress without detection.
Quarterly review of finding category distributions across experiments reveals systemic architectural gaps that individual experiment findings do not surface individually.
Experiment reports contain vulnerability information and must be access-controlled with the same discipline as security vulnerability disclosures.
